rationales.power: He fixates on being interrupted "mid-sentence" and on how "if it were Thomas or Sven" nobody would have done that, showing how much deference in the room matters to him.
rationales.conformity: He says meetings should have "a basic level of order" and that "the chair should have handled it," grounding the episode in rule-bound procedure rather than improvisation.

rationales.power: Being sought out at lunch because someone "values your input enough to come find you" matters more to him than a Teams thumbs-up, showing a strong pull toward having his judgment treated as meaningful.
He is upset that he has "no real authority over the things that actually affect our deadlines" and that "someone else holds the pen," making control over outcomes the clear stake.

rationales.achievement: He "spent the weekend on the risk matrix" and comes back to having done "the analysis" thoroughly, then feels stung that none of that work was engaged with.
rationales.power: What hurts is that "Thomas has been here longer, has a louder voice" and the room defaulted to him, showing how much weight and voice in the hierarchy matter here.
He dwells on being left off the "senior management pipeline" and fears the people who decide these things no longer see him as "someone on the upward track," which centers standing and advancement.

rationales.achievement: He is bothered that people praised Clara for "initiative" when "that was mine," showing a strong need for his contribution to be recognized.
rationales.power: He frames the loss as "I set the direction" and "allocated the resource," then feels "invisible in my own team" when that leadership role is not acknowledged.

rationales.benevolence: He spends the one-on-one helping Daan map trade-offs until "he had a clear plan," and says that kind of problem-solving with another person is "the version of my job I actually like."

rationales.self_direction: She says the house seems to demand "this complete surrender of any time that's mine" and then scrubs off the indigo even though it felt "like erasing something," showing her own path getting suppressed.
rationales.conformity: She removes the stains because it was "easier than sitting through another silent dinner" and says she "pick[s] my fights," prioritizing smooth relations over pushing back.

rationales.self_direction: Her first instinct is "my son likes drawing, why is that a problem?" and she resents that "everyone has an opinion ... except the boy himself," clearly siding with his own preferences.
rationales.conformity: In the meeting she "smiled and nodded" and promised to push him toward being "more social," and later says "you just don't" challenge it there, showing deference to the setting's expectations.

rationales.tradition: Making aloo paratha "the way Appa used to" and being carried back to the old house in Mysore shows deliberate connection to inherited family practice.

rationales.self_direction: The indigo work gives her "two hours of silence" with her "hands doing what they wanted to do," and she "didn't even check my phone," signaling protected creative autonomy.
rationales.tradition: She helps set up the Diwali puja and makes rangoli "the way my grandmother taught me," explicitly choosing inherited methods over "acrylic paint shortcuts."
She agrees to teach Vihaan the Sahasranama because "it's important" and says she's glad Appa made sure she knew it "by heart," treating the practice as something worth carrying forward.
She teaches the verses with their meaning and describes it as "this thread that connects us backward through generations," explicitly preserving inherited practice.
rationales.benevolence: She wants Vihaan to feel "the warmth of sitting together with something that matters" and shapes the lesson around patience rather than pressure.

rationales.self_direction: The dye work is fully hers, from a fermentation vat she "built" to deciding whether to take Priya's order, and she is actively protecting time and space to pursue it.
rationales.achievement: She catalogs "fourteen shades of indigo," labels mordants and dip counts, and thinks through whether her setup can handle "twelve table runners, twenty napkins," showing disciplined craft and execution.

rationales.self_direction: She claims those afternoon hours as "mine" and rejects the idea that they are "leftover time that belongs to the house," which is a clear defense of her own lane.
When Ravi calls it "just a small project," she says nothing, clears the plates, and goes upstairs, letting her own aim be diminished in the moment.
rationales.conformity: She swallows her response at dinner and keeps the evening moving by "finish[ing] dinner" and "clear[ing] the plates," choosing social smoothness over confrontation.

rationales.self_direction: She wants "not the permission, not the approval" but "the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine," which is an explicit claim to make this work on her own terms.
She pushes back on the idea that every hour with indigo is "stolen" and notes that "nobody told Ravi he has to choose," showing resistance to externally imposed limits on how she lives.
rationales.tradition: She takes the boys to the "monthly abhishekam," notes Vihaan reciting the Sahasranama, and feels proud when the priest says they are "keeping these things alive."

rationales.self_direction: She accepts the order despite fear and lands on "I know I'm doing my thing," making an explicit choice to back her own path.
rationales.achievement: Saying yes to "twelve runners, twenty napkins" with a specific "kalamkari-inspired border block" shows a concrete commitment to producing and delivering skilled work.
